section.title: Ahaz of Judah
section.sectionRationale: Ahaz breaks sharply from his father Jotham's relative faithfulness, sacrificing his own son in the fire and adopting Canaanite worship practices. Under military pressure from Aram and Israel, he buys Assyrian help with temple and palace treasures, then, having seen a pagan altar in Damascus, has a matching altar built in the LORD's own temple and rearranges Israel's worship to suit his political and religious compromise.
section.thesis: Ahaz's willingness to sacrifice his own son, buy foreign protection with the LORD's treasures, and redesign true worship after a pagan pattern shows a king who has fully inverted covenant priorities, trusting Assyria and imitating idolatry rather than trusting the LORD who had preserved David's line for exactly this kind of crisis.
section.frameNote: Three beats: Ahaz's idolatry and appeal to Assyria for help, verses 1 to 9; his substitute altar modeled on Damascus, verses 10 to 16; and his further stripping and rearranging of the temple's furnishings, verses 17 to 20.

units.label: Ahaz's idolatry and appeal to Assyria
units.note: I am your servant and your son

verses.text: In the seventeenth year of Pekah son of Remaliah, Ahaz son of Jotham became king of Judah.
Ahaz was twenty years old when he became king, and he reigned in Jerusalem sixteen years. And unlike David his father, he did not do what was right in the eyes of the LORD his God.
Instead, he walked in the ways of the kings of Israel and even sacrificed his son in the {{esh|fire}}, according to the abominations of the nations that the LORD had driven out before the Israelites.
And he sacrificed and burned incense on the {{bamah|high places}}, on the hills, and under every green tree.
Then Rezin king of Aram and Pekah son of Remaliah king of Israel came up to wage war against Jerusalem. They besieged Ahaz but could not overcome him.
At that time Rezin king of Aram recovered Elath for Aram, drove out the men of Judah, and sent the Edomites into Elath, where they live to this day.

So Ahaz sent messengers to Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria, saying, "I am your {{eved|servant}} and your son. Come up and save me from the hands of the kings of Aram and Israel, who are rising up against me."
Ahaz also took the silver and gold found in the house of the LORD and in the treasuries of the king's palace, and he sent it as a gift to the king of Assyria.
So the king of Assyria responded to him, marched up to Damascus, and captured it. He took its people to Kir as captives and put Rezin to death.

Then King Ahaz went to Damascus to meet Tiglath-pileser king of Assyria. On seeing the altar in Damascus, King Ahaz sent Uriah the priest a model of the {{mizbeach|altar}} and complete plans for its construction.
And Uriah the priest built the altar according to all the instructions King Ahaz had sent from Damascus, and he completed it before King Ahaz returned.
When the king came back from Damascus and saw the altar, he approached it and presented offerings on it.
He offered his burnt offering and his grain offering, poured out his drink offering, and splattered the blood of his peace offerings on the altar.
He also took the bronze altar that stood before the LORD from the front of the temple (between the new altar and the house of the LORD) and he put it on the north side of the new altar.

Then King Ahaz commanded Uriah the priest, "Offer on the great altar the morning burnt offering, the evening grain offering, and the king's burnt offering and grain offering, as well as the burnt offerings, grain offerings, and drink offerings of all the people of the land. Splatter on the altar all the blood of the burnt offerings and sacrifices. But I will use the bronze altar to seek guidance."
So Uriah the priest did just as King Ahaz had commanded.

units.label: Further stripping of the temple
units.note: on account of the king of Assyria

verses.text: King Ahaz also cut off the frames of the movable stands and removed the bronze basin from each of them. He took down the Sea from the bronze oxen that were under it and put it on a stone base.
And on account of the king of Assyria, he removed the Sabbath canopy they had built in the temple and closed the royal entryway outside the house of the LORD.
As for the rest of the acts of Ahaz, along with his accomplishments, are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?
And Ahaz rested with his fathers and was buried with them in the City of David, and his son Hezekiah reigned in his place.

esh.gloss: Fire. Ahaz's act of making his own son pass through the esh represents child sacrifice, the very abomination the LORD had specifically warned against, Leviticus 18:21, Deuteronomy 18:10, marking the depth of Ahaz's departure from covenant faithfulness even beyond his predecessors.

bamah.gloss: High place, an elevated open-air worship site often associated with Canaanite fertility religion. While earlier kings tolerated existing bamot without removing them, Ahaz actively sacrifices and burns incense on them himself, personally participating in what previous kings had merely failed to eliminate.

mizbeach.gloss: Altar, the place of sacrifice. Ahaz replaces the LORD's own bronze altar, made under Solomon's original temple design, with a mizbeach copied from a pagan Assyrian or Aramean pattern seen in Damascus, physically relocating the true altar and demoting it to a place for merely seeking guidance rather than covenant sacrifice.

eved.gloss: Servant or vassal. Ahaz's self-description to Tiglath-pileser, I am your eved and your son, willingly submits Judah's king to a foreign overlord's authority, a stark reversal of Judah's proper identity as servant to the LORD alone and heir to David's covenant promises.

basic.context: Ahaz breaks decisively from his father Jotham's relative faithfulness, sacrificing his own son in the fire and adopting the worship practices of the pagan nations Israel had displaced. When Aram and Israel besiege Jerusalem, rather than trusting the LORD, Ahaz strips the temple and palace treasuries to buy Assyrian intervention, explicitly calling himself Assyria's servant and son. After personally visiting Damascus and admiring a pagan altar there, he has an exact copy built in the LORD's own temple, relegates the original bronze altar to a secondary role, and further rearranges and strips temple furnishings to appease his new Assyrian overlord.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative describing one particularly compromised king's specific choices; it is not a general commentary on international alliances or temple architecture. What endures is the sobering pattern that trusting foreign power over the LORD, and reshaping true worship to accommodate that trust, represents a fundamental inversion of covenant priorities with serious consequences.
basic.originalLanguages: Ahaz makes his own son pass through esh, fire, the very child sacrifice the LORD had specifically forbidden, and personally worships at the bamah, high places, rather than merely tolerating them. He calls himself Assyria's eved, servant, rather than the LORD's, and replaces the temple's true mizbeach, altar, with a design copied directly from pagan Damascus, demoting the LORD's own altar to a lesser function.
basic.typology: Ahaz's substitution of a pagan-patterned altar for the LORD's own prescribed altar, and his willing submission to a foreign power as servant and son, anticipates the New Testament's warning against conforming worship and identity to the patterns of the world rather than to God's own revealed order.
basic.christ: Ahaz's willingness to sacrifice his own son to a foreign god stands in the most jarring possible contrast to the LORD's own willingness to give His only Son, not to appease a rival power but to accomplish salvation, John 3:16, showing that the true and living God requires no such horror, having Himself provided the ultimate, sufficient sacrifice in Christ.
basic.god: God had explicitly forbidden child sacrifice and the adoption of pagan worship patterns precisely because He alone is worthy of Israel's trust and exclusive worship; Ahaz's choices represent a direct, personal violation of both. Even so, the LORD preserves David's line through Ahaz's son Hezekiah, showing His covenant faithfulness continuing despite the unfaithfulness of an individual king.
basic.crossReferences: Leviticus 18:21 and Deuteronomy 18:10 explicitly forbid the child sacrifice Ahaz commits. Isaiah 7:1-12 records the LORD's own offer of a sign to Ahaz during this very crisis, an offer Ahaz refuses out of false humility that masks unbelief, choosing to trust Assyria instead. 2 Chronicles 28 gives a fuller account of Ahaz's reign, including further military disasters that befell Judah during this period of unfaithfulness.
basic.oneStory: Ahaz's willingness to sacrifice his own son, trust a foreign empire over the LORD, and remake true worship after a pagan pattern represents one of the darkest turns in Judah's kings, previewing the depths of covenant unfaithfulness that will eventually bring exile, while standing in complete contrast to the true God who gives, rather than demands, His own Son to save His people.

context.literary: The chapter moves from a general summary of Ahaz's idolatry, to the specific crisis of foreign invasion and his response, to the detailed and almost methodical account of his temple modifications, building a portrait of a king whose compromise touches every level, personal, political, and liturgical.
context.historical: The Syro-Ephraimite crisis, the combined attack of Aram, Syria, and Israel, Ephraim, against Judah, is independently corroborated by Assyrian records and forms the backdrop of Isaiah's famous prophecy to Ahaz in Isaiah 7. Tiglath-pileser III's own Assyrian annals record receiving tribute from Judah's king Ahaz by name, and the practice of copying architectural or religious models seen abroad, as Ahaz does with the Damascus altar, reflects genuine ancient royal customs of cultural exchange, here turned toward idolatrous compromise.
context.canonical: This chapter's crisis directly frames Isaiah's ministry to Ahaz in Isaiah 7 to 8, including the famous Immanuel prophecy given precisely because Ahaz refused to ask the LORD for a sign, and it sets up the sharp contrast with Ahaz's son Hezekiah, whose faithful reign in chapters 18 to 20 reverses many of his father's compromises.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To record Ahaz's uniquely severe covenant unfaithfulness, including child sacrifice, foreign political dependence, and the wholesale redesign of temple worship after a pagan pattern, showing the depths to which a Davidic king could fall even while God preserved the covenant line through his successor.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter narrates one particular king's specific and severe choices; it does not prescribe or evaluate general principles of international relations or architectural imitation as inherently wrong, but it does clearly condemn adopting pagan religious practices and trusting foreign power over the LORD as a substitute for covenant faithfulness.

debates.question: Was Ahaz's appeal to Assyria for military help, verse 7, itself sinful, or was the deeper problem his broader idolatry and altar substitution?

views.label: The appeal to Assyria was itself a serious failure of trust in the LORD
views.case: Isaiah 7:1-12 records the LORD offering Ahaz a sign of deliverance through trusting Him rather than a foreign power, an offer Ahaz explicitly refuses under a veneer of false piety, suggesting the appeal to Assyria itself, not merely its aftermath, constituted a rejection of the LORD's sufficient protection for Jerusalem, a rejection Isaiah pointedly condemns.

views.label: The appeal to Assyria was a a common ancient diplomatic practice, but Ahaz's subsequent religious compromise was the deeper and clearer sin
views.case: Seeking foreign military alliance under existential threat was a widespread and not inherently forbidden practice among ancient Near Eastern kings; the text's specific, detailed condemnation focuses more explicitly on Ahaz's personal idolatry, child sacrifice, and his subsequent religious innovations at the temple, suggesting these, rather than the alliance itself, are presented as the chapter's central moral failures.

debates.bearing: Both readings agree that Ahaz's fundamental error was failing to trust the LORD as Judah's true defender, whether that failure is located primarily in the initial appeal to Assyria, the refusal of Isaiah's offered sign, or the subsequent wholesale religious compromise that followed.

study.originalLanguages: Ahaz commits the gravest possible covenant violation, making his own son pass through esh, fire, and personally worshiping at every bamah, high place, rather than merely tolerating them as prior kings had. He calls himself Assyria's eved, servant, rather than owning his true identity as servant of the LORD and heir of David, and he replaces the temple's God-ordained mizbeach, altar, with a design copied from pagan Damascus, relegating the LORD's own altar to a secondary role reserved merely for seeking guidance.

typology.type: A king who sacrifices his own son to a foreign god rather than trusting the LORD
typology.fulfillment: The stark contrast of the true God, who gives His own Son to save, not appease, His people, John 3:16.

typology.type: True worship reshaped after a pagan pattern seen abroad rather than God's own revealed design
typology.fulfillment: The New Testament warning against conforming to the patterns of this world rather than being transformed according to God's will, Romans 12:2.

study.christ: Ahaz's horrifying willingness to sacrifice his own son to a foreign god stands in starkest possible contrast to the LORD, who so loved the world that He gave His only Son, not to appease a rival power out of fear, but freely, to accomplish the salvation of His people, John 3:16. Where Ahaz redesigned true worship after a pagan pattern seen in Damascus, Christ is the true and perfect fulfillment of every sacrifice and altar, requiring no imitation of foreign models, since He Himself is the once-for-all, sufficient offering, Hebrews 10:10-14.
study.god: God had explicitly and repeatedly forbidden both child sacrifice and the adoption of pagan worship patterns precisely because He alone is worthy of Israel's complete trust and exclusive worship, and Ahaz's choices represent a direct, deeply personal violation of both commands. Even so, God's covenant faithfulness to David's line continues undeterred, preserving the throne for Ahaz's far more faithful son Hezekiah, showing that His purposes are never ultimately thwarted by even the worst individual unfaithfulness.

crossReferences.note: Explicitly forbids giving children to be passed through the fire, the exact abomination Ahaz commits with his own son.
Records the LORD's gracious offer of a sign to Ahaz during this very crisis, an offer he refuses, choosing to trust Assyria instead.
Gives a fuller account of Ahaz's reign, including further disasters and his continued idolatry despite worsening circumstances.
Warns against conforming to the patterns of this world, the very failure Ahaz commits by remodeling true worship after pagan Damascus.

study.oneStory: Ahaz's willingness to sacrifice his own son, place his trust in a foreign empire rather than the LORD, and remake true worship after a pagan pattern marks one of the darkest turns among Judah's kings, previewing the depths of covenant unfaithfulness that will eventually bring the nation to exile. Yet even here, God's covenant promise to David quietly continues through Hezekiah, and the whole scene stands in vivid contrast to the true God, who gives rather than demands His own Son, to save rather than merely to appease.

quiz.q: What especially severe act of idolatry did Ahaz commit that even many earlier kings had not done?

options.text: He sacrificed his own son in the fire, following the abominable practices of the nations the LORD had driven out.
options.note: Verse 3 records this specific and severe act of child sacrifice.

options.text: He simply tolerated the existing high places without removing them.
options.note: This was common among many kings; Ahaz's distinctive severity was personally sacrificing his son and worshiping actively at the high places himself.

options.text: He refused to build any altars at all during his reign.
options.note: The text describes Ahaz actively building and using altars, including a pagan-patterned one, not refusing to build any.

options.text: He had no involvement in idolatrous worship whatsoever.
options.note: The text explicitly and extensively describes his personal idolatrous practices.

quiz.q: How did Ahaz respond to the combined threat from Aram and Israel against Jerusalem?

options.text: He sent Assyria's king tribute from the temple and palace treasuries, calling himself Assyria's servant and son and asking for military rescue.
options.note: Verses 7 to 8 record this exact appeal and payment to Tiglath-pileser.

options.text: He sought the LORD's guidance through the prophet Isaiah and trusted in divine deliverance.
options.note: Isaiah 7 records the LORD offering Ahaz a sign of deliverance, which he refused, choosing instead to appeal to Assyria.

options.text: He surrendered Jerusalem immediately without resistance.
options.note: The text states Jerusalem was besieged but not overcome, and Ahaz actively sought foreign intervention rather than surrendering.

options.text: He successfully defeated both Aram and Israel through his own army.
options.note: The text describes Ahaz relying on Assyrian intervention rather than an independent military victory.

quiz.q: What did Ahaz do after seeing an altar in Damascus during his visit to Tiglath-pileser?

options.text: He sent plans for a matching altar to the priest Uriah, who built an exact copy in the LORD's temple before Ahaz's return.
options.note: Verses 10 to 11 record this exact sequence of events.

options.text: He condemned the Damascus altar as idolatrous and refused to replicate it.
options.note: The text describes Ahaz actively commissioning an exact replica, not condemning or rejecting the design.

options.text: He destroyed the original bronze altar entirely rather than relocating it.
options.note: The original altar was relocated to the north side rather than destroyed, verse 14.

options.text: Uriah the priest refused to build the new altar as commanded.
options.note: The text explicitly states Uriah built the altar exactly as instructed.

quiz.q: What became of the original bronze altar after the new altar was installed?

options.text: It was moved to the north side of the new altar and relegated to a secondary role, used by the king merely for seeking guidance.
options.note: Verses 14 to 15 record this exact relocation and diminished function.

options.text: It remained the primary altar used for all sacrifices as before.
options.note: The text explicitly states the new altar became the primary altar for regular sacrifices instead.

options.text: It was sent to Assyria as tribute along with other temple treasures.
options.note: The bronze altar was relocated within the temple grounds, not sent away as tribute.

options.text: It was left exactly where it had always stood with no change.
options.note: The text explicitly describes its relocation to the north side of the new altar.

quiz.q: What other temple modifications did Ahaz make, and for what stated reason?

options.text: He removed the Sabbath canopy and closed the royal entryway to the temple, explicitly on account of the king of Assyria.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record these specific changes and their explicit connection to appeasing Assyria.

options.text: He expanded the temple significantly to accommodate more worshipers.
options.note: The described changes involved removing and altering existing furnishings, not expanding the temple's capacity.

options.text: He restored all the temple furnishings to their original condition.
options.note: The text describes further stripping and alteration of furnishings, not restoration to original condition.

options.text: No further changes to the temple are recorded beyond the altar.
options.note: Verses 17 to 18 record additional specific modifications beyond the altar itself.

questions: Ahaz called himself Assyria's servant and son rather than trusting his true identity as the LORD's covenant king. Where might you be seeking security from something other than God rather than trusting your true identity in Him?

Ahaz saw something impressive abroad and immediately wanted to import it into true worship, reshaping God's pattern to match the world's. Where might you be tempted to reshape your own faith or worship to match what looks appealing in the culture around you rather than what God has revealed?

Ahaz's compromises touched his personal life, his politics, and his worship all at once. How connected are these areas in your own life, and where might compromise in one area be quietly spreading into the others?

Even amid Ahaz's severe unfaithfulness, God's covenant promise continued through his son Hezekiah. Where do you need to trust that God's larger purposes are not derailed by even serious failures around you?

Ahaz refused to ask God for a sign of deliverance, preferring self-reliance dressed up as humility. Where might false humility be keeping you from asking God directly for the help or assurance you actually need?

translationNotes.note: The phrase made his son pass through the fire is a specific technical description of child sacrifice, explicitly forbidden in Leviticus 18:21 and associated with the worship of Molech and similar deities.

The BSB footnote notes Aram may alternately be rendered Syria, and Elath was a strategic port city on the Gulf of Aqaba whose loss to Edom represented a real and significant territorial and economic setback for Judah.

The BSB footnote allows the Sabbath canopy to be alternately rendered as the base of the king's throne, reflecting some uncertainty in the precise nature of this particular structure removed to appease Assyria.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for 2 Kings 16:1-20. No em dashes or en dashes appear in the source text for this chapter.
